meta.certificationName: Databricks Certified Generative AI Engineer Associate
meta.title: Mock Exam 4

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team is using an LLM-as-judge to evaluate factual accuracy of RAG responses. They want to make the judge's scoring more reliable and consistent. Which prompt design technique is MOST effective?

options.text: Ask the judge to provide a score on a 1-100 scale for finer granularity
Provide the judge with a detailed rubric that includes concrete examples of what constitutes each score level, anchored to specific failure modes
Ask the judge to score multiple responses simultaneously to improve consistency
Use a lower temperature for the generator LLM so the judge receives more consistent responses to evaluate

questions.explanation: B is correct here because LLM judges without explicit rubrics suffer from high variance and positivity bias. A well-designed rubric that defines each score level with concrete examples (e.g., 'Score 1: The response contradicts a fact stated in the context', 'Score 5: All claims are directly supported by the retrieved context with no additions') anchors the judge's scoring to observable criteria, dramatically improving reliability and consistency. A is wrong because finer-grained scales (1-100) increase variance without improving reliability. C is wrong because batch evaluation can introduce order effects and is not a standard technique for improving consistency. D is wrong because the generator's temperature does not affect how the judge is prompted.
questions.reference: LLM-as-judge evaluation: prompt construction for evaluation

questions.stem: A team is setting up production monitoring for a RAG-based application. Select TWO metrics that are MOST important to track for operational health.

options.text: End-to-end query latency (p50, p95, p99) to detect performance degradation
The Pearson correlation coefficient between embedding dimensions
Token usage per query to track and control inference costs
The L2 norm of each document's embedding vector

questions.explanation: A is correct here because latency is a primary operational health metric — sudden increases in p95 or p99 latency indicate problems with the retrieval index, the LLM endpoint, or the overall chain. C is correct because LLM calls are priced per token, and tracking token usage per query enables cost management, anomaly detection (unusually large prompts), and budget forecasting. B is wrong because the correlation between embedding dimensions is an internal model property that does not reflect application health. D is wrong because the L2 norm of individual embedding vectors is a mathematical property with no direct operational significance in production monitoring.
questions.reference: Production monitoring: latency tracking, cost per query, token usage metrics

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team notices that users who ask vague, short queries (e.g., 'fix it', 'explain more') consistently receive poor RAG responses. The team wants to improve responses to these queries without changing the chunking strategy or embedding model. Which approach is MOST targeted?

options.text: Increase top-k from 3 to 15 for all queries to ensure vague queries retrieve at least some relevant content
Apply HyDE: use an LLM to generate a hypothetical answer for the vague query, then use that hypothetical text as the vector search query instead of the original short query
Reject queries shorter than 10 words and prompt users to rephrase
Switch from ANN to exact search so that short queries do not miss any indexed documents

questions.explanation: B is correct here because HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) specifically targets the problem of sparse, short, or vague queries that embed poorly. By prompting an LLM to generate a plausible hypothetical answer to 'fix it' or 'explain more' using the conversation history, the resulting text is rich with domain vocabulary and sentence structure similar to actual documents in the index, producing a much better query vector. A increases recall but at the cost of precision — sending 15 chunks to the LLM for every query is expensive and often noisy. C degrades user experience and ignores legitimate short queries. D switches search algorithms but does not help when the query embedding itself is semantically poor.
questions.reference: RAG architecture: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) for sparse query improvement

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team is preparing metadata to enable filtered search in their RAG system. They want users to be able to filter retrieved documents by 'department' and 'document_date'. What is required to enable these filters in Databricks Vector Search?

options.text: Create separate vector indexes for each department to enable isolation
Include 'department' and 'document_date' as columns in the source Delta table, and specify them as filterable columns when creating the Vector Search index
Append the department name and date to the text of each chunk before embedding so the embedding captures this information
Use a WHERE clause in the SQL query that creates the source Delta table to pre-filter documents

questions.explanation: B is correct here because Databricks Vector Search supports structured metadata filtering, but the filterable columns must be explicitly declared at index creation time. The metadata columns ('department', 'document_date') must exist in the source Delta table and be registered as filter columns when creating the index. At query time, the filters are passed as key-value pairs to similarity_search(). A is wrong because creating separate indexes per department is operationally complex and does not support cross-department searches. C is a workaround (called 'stuffing metadata into text') that degrades embedding quality without providing reliable filter semantics. D is wrong because the Delta table itself is not being queried with SQL at search time.
questions.reference: Data quality for RAG: metadata extraction for filtering

questions.stem: In this scenario, a pipeline ingests HTML web pages for a RAG knowledge base. The HTML contains navigation menus, footers, cookie banners, and advertisement content alongside the main article text. What preprocessing step is MOST important?

options.text: Use beautiful soup or a similar HTML parser to extract only the main article body and strip navigation, footer, and advertisement elements
Embed the raw HTML including all tags so the embedding model can use structural signals
Split the HTML at every <div> tag boundary to create chunks
Convert the HTML to uppercase before parsing to normalize case

questions.explanation: A is correct here because navigation menus, footers, and ads are present on every page and contain no information relevant to the main topic. Embedding them pollutes the index with repeated boilerplate that will superficially match many queries (e.g., a nav menu with 'Home, Products, Support' might match support-related queries) while providing no useful context. Parsing tools like BeautifulSoup, Trafilatura, or Unstructured can identify and extract the main content. B is wrong because HTML tags add noise and most embedding models are not trained to interpret HTML structure meaningfully. C is wrong because <div> boundaries do not align with semantic content boundaries.
questions.reference: Document loaders: loading HTML, extracting text

questions.stem: A team needs to add invisible watermarks to text generated by their LLM application so that AI-generated content can be detected later, even if the text is slightly modified. What technique achieves this?

options.text: Add a visible disclaimer at the end of every LLM response indicating it was AI-generated
Use a text watermarking algorithm that subtly biases the LLM's token selection (e.g., using a green/red token list) to embed a detectable statistical pattern in the output text
Log every LLM response to a Delta table so the original can be retrieved and compared
Use a hash of each response as a digital signature and append it to the text

questions.explanation: B is correct here because LLM watermarking algorithms (such as the Kirchenbauer et al. 2023 method) work by partitioning the vocabulary into 'green' and 'red' token lists at generation time using a secret key and cryptographic hash of the preceding token. The LLM is biased toward selecting green tokens, embedding a statistically detectable pattern that survives moderate paraphrasing. A is visible disclosure, not watermarking, and is easily removed. C maintains a log for auditing but is not a watermark in the generated text itself — the text cannot be identified as AI-generated without access to the log. D appends a hash that is trivially removed by deleting the signature.
questions.reference: Responsible AI: watermarking AI-generated content

questions.stem: A team needs to use Databricks Foundation Model APIs to generate text embeddings for their RAG pipeline without deploying their own model. Which model is available as a managed embedding endpoint in Databricks Foundation Model APIs?

options.text: text-embedding-ada-002 (OpenAI)
databricks-bge-large-en
sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (local HuggingFace)
cohere-embed-english-v3.0

questions.explanation: B is correct here because databricks-bge-large-en is one of the embedding models available as a managed Foundation Model API endpoint in Databricks — accessible via the mlflow.deployments client or the REST API without any model deployment configuration. A (text-embedding-ada-002) is an OpenAI model accessible via their API or through the Databricks AI Gateway routing to OpenAI, not a native Databricks Foundation Model. C is a HuggingFace model that requires custom deployment. D is a Cohere model accessible via AI Gateway or directly through Cohere's API.
questions.reference: Embedding models in Databricks: bge-large, Databricks Foundation Model APIs

questions.stem: In this scenario, a GenAI application uses the self-consistency technique to reduce hallucination: it generates 5 responses to the same query and returns the answer that appears most frequently. What is the PRIMARY limitation of this approach in production?

options.text: Self-consistency cannot work with RAG because retrieval changes between calls
Generating 5 responses multiplies inference cost and latency by 5x, making it expensive for high-throughput applications
Self-consistency guarantees correctness only if at least 4 of 5 responses agree
Self-consistency causes the model to forget the system prompt after multiple calls

questions.explanation: B is correct here because self-consistency is a hallucination mitigation technique where multiple independent samples are generated and the majority answer is selected. While effective, it comes with a direct 5x cost and latency multiplier — each additional sample requires a full LLM inference pass. For latency-sensitive or high-volume applications, this overhead may be unacceptable. In practice, self-consistency is used selectively for high-stakes queries where correctness outweighs cost. A is wrong because self-consistency is compatible with RAG; the same retrieved context can be included in all 5 prompts. C is wrong because there is no fixed threshold — the technique uses majority vote, but correctness is not mathematically guaranteed. D is wrong because system prompts are included in each call independently.
questions.reference: Hallucination mitigation: self-consistency

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team deploys two versions of their RAG system's system prompt (version A and version B) simultaneously, routing 50% of traffic to each. They measure RAGAS faithfulness and user satisfaction for both over two weeks. What technique is this?

options.text: Shadow deployment, where version B receives traffic but its responses are discarded
A/B testing of prompts, where two prompt variants are compared on live traffic using quantitative metrics
Canary deployment, where version B receives 1% of traffic as a safety check before full rollout
Blue-green deployment, where version B is deployed to a separate environment and receives no production traffic

questions.explanation: B is correct here because routing 50% of traffic to each prompt variant and measuring outcomes (RAGAS metrics, user satisfaction) over a sustained period is a classic A/B test — the prompt variants are the treatment and control conditions. This allows the team to make a data-driven decision about which prompt performs better. A (shadow deployment) routes traffic to a new version but discards its responses, used for testing stability not quality comparison. C (canary) sends only a small slice of traffic (1-10%) to the new version to limit risk exposure, not to measure performance parity. D (blue-green) deploys to a separate environment with no production traffic.
questions.reference: Maintaining applications: prompt versioning and A/B testing prompts

questions.stem: In this scenario, a developer is writing a few-shot prompt for an LLM that classifies customer support tickets by urgency. The prompt includes 5 labeled examples before the user's input. What is the purpose of these few-shot examples?

options.text: To fine-tune the model on the provided examples before each inference call
To demonstrate the expected input format and output style to the model within the context window, guiding it to follow the same pattern for the new input
To increase the context window size available to the model
To replace the system prompt so that the model ignores its original instructions

questions.explanation: B is correct here because few-shot prompting uses labeled examples within the context to show the model the expected input-output pattern. The model uses these examples to infer the task format and style, improving the consistency and accuracy of its response for the new input without any weight updates. A is wrong because few-shot examples do not modify model weights — in-context learning is purely inference-time. C is wrong because context window size is a fixed model property that examples do not change. D is wrong because few-shot examples complement the system prompt rather than replacing it.
questions.reference: Prompt templates: few-shot examples

questions.stem: In this scenario, an engineer is evaluating whether to use the Databricks Foundation Model APIs (pay-per-token) or deploy their own Llama 3 70B on provisioned throughput for a high-volume application processing 50,000 requests per day with an average of 2,000 tokens per request. The cost per token on the Foundation Model API is $0.002 per 1K tokens. A dedicated A100 GPU instance costs $8/hour. Which option is likely more cost-effective at this volume?

options.text: Foundation Model API pay-per-token, because it eliminates infrastructure management overhead
Provisioned throughput with a dedicated A100 instance, because at this token volume the fixed hourly instance cost is lower than the per-token cost
Foundation Model API, because provisioned throughput cannot serve 50,000 requests per day
They are equivalent in cost at this volume

questions.explanation: B is correct here. Daily token volume = 50,000 requests × 2,000 tokens = 100,000,000 tokens = 100,000K tokens. At $0.002/1K tokens, daily Foundation Model API cost = 100,000 × $0.002 = $200/day. A dedicated A100 GPU at $8/hour = $192/day. At this volume, the dedicated instance ($192/day) is already cheaper than the per-token cost ($200/day), and the gap grows with volume. Additionally, provisioned throughput provides guaranteed latency. A is wrong because while infrastructure management is reduced with pay-per-token, the cost economics favor provisioned throughput at this scale. C is wrong because there is no such capability limit.
questions.reference: Model serving for LLMs: provisioned throughput vs pay-per-token; Foundation Model APIs

questions.stem: In this scenario, a Databricks engineer is configuring a Databricks Vector Search index. The documents are updated continuously in a Delta table, and the engineer wants index changes to propagate automatically. Which index type should they choose?

options.text: Direct Vector Access index
Delta Sync index
Flat L2 index
HNSW approximate index

questions.explanation: B is correct here because a Delta Sync index automatically syncs with the source Delta table when it changes, including through triggered or continuous pipeline mode. A is wrong because a Direct Vector Access index requires the caller to manage upserts/deletes to the index directly via API — there is no automatic sync from a Delta table. C and D are wrong because these describe similarity algorithm types, not Databricks Vector Search index types.
questions.reference: Vector search design: Databricks Vector Search index types (delta sync vs direct vector access)

questions.stem: In this scenario, a data scientist is comparing Llama 3 8B and Llama 3 70B for a RAG chatbot. The 8B model is significantly cheaper but the team is concerned about response quality. For which type of query would the 8B model be MOST likely to underperform relative to the 70B model?

options.text: Simple factual lookups where the answer is directly present in the retrieved context
Queries requiring multi-step reasoning, synthesis across multiple retrieved passages, or complex instruction following
Queries that require generating short, single-sentence responses
Queries submitted in English about well-known general topics

questions.explanation: B is correct here because larger models have a significantly greater capacity for complex reasoning, instruction following, and synthesis tasks. For simple fact retrieval where the answer appears verbatim in the context, an 8B model often performs comparably to a 70B model. Multi-step reasoning, synthesis across documents, and nuanced instruction following are where scale differences are most pronounced. A, C, and D describe straightforward tasks where the smaller model is likely to perform acceptably.
questions.reference: LLM selection: model size tradeoffs (latency/cost/quality)

questions.stem: In this scenario, a production RAG application has a Databricks Vector Search Delta Sync index. The source Delta table is updated nightly via a batch job. The team finds that queries executed immediately after the batch job still return old results. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The embedding model is cached and needs to be reloaded after the source table changes
The Delta Sync index has not yet completed its sync cycle after the batch update; the index refresh may be triggered or scheduled with a delay
Databricks Vector Search always requires a 24-hour propagation delay for new documents
The vector index must be dropped and recreated from scratch after every source table update

questions.explanation: B is correct here because Delta Sync indexes do not sync instantaneously — they run on a pipeline that detects changes via the Delta table's Change Data Feed and processes them. If the batch job completes and queries are issued before the sync pipeline has processed the new rows, stale results will be returned. The team should trigger a manual sync after the batch job completes, or configure the pipeline trigger to start immediately after the batch. C is wrong because there is no fixed 24-hour delay — refresh speed depends on pipeline mode and data volume. D is wrong because Delta Sync indexes support incremental updates and do not need to be rebuilt from scratch.
questions.reference: Index maintenance: Delta Sync index auto-refresh triggers, manual refresh

questions.stem: In this scenario, a startup wants to deploy a GenAI application with the lowest possible inference cost and does not require top-tier response quality. Which LLM selection strategy is MOST aligned with these goals?

options.text: GPT-4 via Databricks AI Gateway for maximum quality
A small open-source model (e.g., Mistral 7B) deployed on Databricks Model Serving with pay-per-token pricing
Claude Opus via Databricks AI Gateway for its large context window
Fine-tune GPT-4 on proprietary data to improve quality and reduce call frequency

questions.explanation: B is correct here because smaller open-source models like Mistral 7B have dramatically lower inference costs and can be hosted on smaller GPU instances. Pay-per-token external models like GPT-4 or Claude Opus (A, C) are expensive for high-volume use cases. D is wrong because GPT-4 fine-tuning is not available in the same way as open-source models and would still be expensive at inference time.
questions.reference: LLM selection: open-source vs proprietary, model size tradeoffs

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team builds a LangChain LCEL chain: retriever | format_docs | prompt | llm | StrOutputParser(). During a load test, they notice that the retriever step is the bottleneck. Which optimization would MOST effectively reduce the retrieval step latency?

options.text: Increase the LLM's max_tokens parameter to generate shorter responses
Switch the Vector Search index from exact search to ANN (approximate nearest neighbor) search to trade a small amount of recall for significantly lower latency
Use a larger embedding model to improve embedding quality
Add more few-shot examples to the prompt template

questions.explanation: B is correct here because the vector search retrieval step's latency is dominated by the search algorithm. Exact search is O(n) in the number of indexed vectors, while ANN algorithms like HNSW run in O(log n) time. Switching from exact to ANN search can reduce retrieval latency by orders of magnitude on large indexes, typically with only a 1-5% reduction in recall. A is wrong because max_tokens controls the generator LLM, not the retriever. C is wrong because a larger embedding model would increase the query embedding computation time, not improve retrieval speed. D is wrong because few-shot examples only affect the LLM prompt, not the retrieval step.
questions.reference: Vector search design: approximate vs exact search

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team discovers that their document corpus contains many near-duplicate product descriptions (slightly reworded versions of the same content). What is the BEST approach to handle this before indexing?

options.text: Index all near-duplicates to maximize recall; the vector search will score them similarly and the top result will still be correct
Apply MinHash LSH or embedding-based deduplication to identify and remove near-duplicate chunks before indexing
Increase the similarity threshold in vector search to filter out redundant results at query time
Use a larger chunk size to merge near-duplicate content into single chunks automatically

questions.explanation: B is correct here because near-duplicates inflate the index unnecessarily, and at query time they consume multiple slots in the top-k results with essentially identical content, crowding out diverse relevant chunks. MinHash LSH is efficient for text deduplication at scale; embedding-based cosine similarity can catch semantic near-duplicates. A is wrong because while the top result may be correct, redundant chunks waste k-slots and reduce the diversity of context provided to the LLM. C is wrong because increasing the similarity threshold changes what is returned but does not eliminate redundancy in the index itself. D is wrong because larger chunk sizes do not merge semantically similar but textually non-adjacent documents.
questions.reference: Data quality for RAG: deduplication

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team is choosing between the BGE-Large and BGE-Small embedding models for their Databricks Vector Search index. BGE-Large produces 1024-dimensional vectors; BGE-Small produces 384-dimensional vectors. Which statement BEST describes the trade-off?

options.text: BGE-Small is always preferable because lower-dimensional embeddings search faster and the quality difference is negligible

BGE-Large produces higher-quality embeddings with better semantic representation, but requires more storage and has higher embedding computation cost; BGE-Small is faster and cheaper but may have lower retrieval quality on complex queries
BGE-Large can only be used with cosine similarity; BGE-Small supports both cosine and dot product
BGE-Small supports longer input sequences than BGE-Large, making it better for long documents

questions.explanation: B is correct here because larger embedding models generally produce richer, more nuanced semantic representations (higher NDCG@10 on retrieval benchmarks), but they cost more to compute and store. BGE-Large at 1024 dimensions requires approximately 2.7x more storage per vector than BGE-Small at 384 dimensions, and embedding computation takes longer. The right choice depends on quality requirements versus cost/latency constraints. A is wrong because the quality difference is not negligible on complex technical queries. C is wrong because both models support standard similarity metrics. D is wrong because maximum input sequence length is a separate property from embedding dimensionality and is similar for both BGE variants.
questions.reference: Embeddings: embedding dimensionality, text-embedding models

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team develops a GenAI application that provides medical symptom information to patients. Which responsible AI practice is MOST critical for this use case?

options.text: Optimizing the model for low latency so patients receive fast responses

Including clear disclaimers that the AI is not a licensed medical professional, recommending users consult a doctor, and implementing content filters to avoid providing specific medical diagnoses or treatment recommendations
Fine-tuning the model on the latest medical journals to maximize accuracy
Using the largest available LLM to maximize knowledge coverage

questions.explanation: B is correct here because in medical applications, the responsible AI priorities are: (1) transparency — users must understand they are interacting with an AI, not a licensed clinician; (2) harm prevention — the system should not provide diagnoses or treatment recommendations that might cause patients to forgo professional care; and (3) appropriate scope — clear guardrails on what the system can and cannot safely assist with. A, C, and D address performance and quality concerns that are secondary to the safety and ethical obligations in medical AI. Fine-tuned accuracy (C) is valuable, but without appropriate disclaimers and scope limitations, even accurate medical information from an AI can be harmful if it leads patients to skip professional consultation.
questions.reference: Content filtering: handling harmful outputs; AI transparency

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team is using LangChain's RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter to chunk a collection of technical manuals. They set chunk_size=512 and chunk_overlap=50. What does the chunk_overlap parameter accomplish?

options.text: It duplicates chunks that appear more than once in the document to improve recall
It ensures that each chunk shares 50 characters with the adjacent chunk, reducing the risk of splitting a sentence or concept across two chunks with no bridging context
It limits the maximum number of overlapping documents returned by the vector search
It sets the number of tokens by which embeddings from adjacent chunks are averaged together

questions.explanation: B is correct here because chunk_overlap causes adjacent chunks to share a specified number of characters (or tokens) at their boundaries. This prevents important information from being lost when a concept or sentence spans a chunk boundary — the repeated text at the overlap ensures both chunks contain the bridging context. A is wrong because deduplication is a separate process. C is wrong because retrieval top-k is a separate parameter. D is wrong because embeddings are generated per chunk independently; they are not averaged with neighbors.
questions.reference: Text splitting: chunk size vs overlap tradeoffs

questions.stem: In this scenario, a developer is configuring the Databricks AI Gateway to route requests to multiple LLM providers. They want certain high-priority enterprise users to always route to GPT-4, while standard users route to a cheaper model. Which Databricks AI Gateway feature supports this?

options.text: Configure separate AI Gateway routes for each user tier, and use application logic to call the appropriate route based on user tier
Enable automatic model selection in the AI Gateway, which reads user role from the JWT token and routes accordingly
Use the AI Gateway's built-in user prioritization feature, which automatically upgrades high-priority users to premium models
Set a higher rate limit for premium users so they automatically receive GPT-4 responses

questions.explanation: A is correct here because the Databricks AI Gateway does not natively route based on user identity — it routes based on the endpoint/route called. The standard pattern for user-tier-based routing is to configure separate routes (e.g., '/gpt4-route' and '/standard-route') and have the application layer select the appropriate route based on the user's tier, which can be determined from authentication metadata (Unity Catalog service principal, user groups, etc.). B is wrong because the AI Gateway does not natively read JWT claims to dynamically select models. C is wrong because no such 'user prioritization' feature exists in the Databricks AI Gateway. D is wrong because rate limits control throughput, not model selection.
questions.reference: MLflow AI Gateway / Databricks AI Gateway: routing, rate limiting

questions.stem: A team is chunking research papers that use a standard academic structure (Abstract, Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion). Select TWO benefits of splitting by section heading rather than by fixed character count.

options.text: Each chunk corresponds to a semantically coherent section, making retrieval more precise — a query about methodology retrieves the Methods section
Section-based chunks guarantee uniform token counts, simplifying batch embedding
Section metadata (e.g., section='Methods') can be stored alongside each chunk for metadata-filtered retrieval
Section-based splitting eliminates the need for any overlap between chunks

questions.explanation: A is correct here because aligning chunks with document sections (Abstract, Methods, Results) creates units that correspond to conceptually distinct parts of the paper. This dramatically improves retrieval precision — a query asking about experimental setup retrieves the Methods section rather than a random character-count slice that spans sections. C is correct because section-based splitting naturally generates section-name metadata that can be attached to each chunk, enabling powerful filtered retrieval (e.g., filter to only search 'Results' sections when a user asks about experimental outcomes). B is wrong because academic sections vary greatly in length — Methods may be 5,000 tokens while the Abstract is 250, so chunk sizes are emphatically not uniform. D is wrong because overlap may still be needed at sub-section boundaries if very long sections are further split.
questions.reference: Chunking strategies: semantic chunking; metadata extraction

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team's RAG system frequently misses relevant documents because user queries use different terminology than the documents (e.g., user asks about 'heart attack' but documents use 'myocardial infarction'). What technique applied BEFORE retrieval would MOST improve recall?

options.text: Reduce the chunk size to increase the number of indexed vectors
Apply query expansion — use an LLM to generate synonyms and related terms for the query, then search using the expanded query or multiple sub-queries
Switch the similarity metric from cosine to dot product
Add a cross-encoder reranker after retrieval to score results more accurately

questions.explanation: B is correct here because query expansion addresses vocabulary mismatch — the gap between how users phrase questions and how documents are written. By prompting an LLM to generate synonyms ('myocardial infarction', 'cardiac event', 'MI') and alternative phrasings, the expanded terms can be embedded and searched, dramatically improving recall for queries that use lay terminology against clinical documents. A increases index density but does not solve vocabulary mismatch. C changes the similarity metric but does not help if the query vector is semantically distant from relevant documents due to vocabulary mismatch. D is a post-retrieval step and cannot retrieve documents that were never in the candidate set.
questions.reference: RAG architecture: query rewriting/expansion before retrieval

questions.stem: A team needs to evaluate whether their RAG system's responses are grounded in the retrieved context (i.e., the answer does not introduce facts not present in the retrieved documents). Which RAGAS metric measures this?

options.text: Answer relevancy
Context recall
Faithfulness
Context precision

questions.explanation: C is correct here because the RAGAS faithfulness metric measures the degree to which each factual claim in the generated answer can be inferred from the retrieved context. A high faithfulness score means the answer is grounded; a low score indicates hallucination. A (answer relevancy) measures whether the answer addresses the user's question. B (context recall) measures whether the relevant information from the ground-truth answer is present in the retrieved context. D (context precision) measures whether the retrieved chunks are relevant to the question (retrieval precision).
questions.reference: RAGAS evaluation metrics: faithfulness

questions.stem: In this scenario, a company trains a new LLM using entirely synthetic data generated by a previous version of the same model. After several such training iterations, the team observes that the model begins to lose diversity and collapses toward repetitive, low-quality outputs. What phenomenon describes this risk?

options.text: Catastrophic forgetting, where the model forgets earlier training data during synthetic data fine-tuning
Model collapse, where iterative training on model-generated synthetic data amplifies errors and reduces output diversity, eventually degrading model quality
Overfitting, where the model memorizes the synthetic data instead of generalizing
Reward hacking, where the model exploits loopholes in the synthetic data generation reward function

questions.explanation: B is correct here because model collapse (sometimes called 'model autophagy disorder') is the documented risk of training iteratively on model-generated synthetic data. Each generation of synthetic data inherits and amplifies the biases and errors of the previous model, while rare or diverse examples are progressively lost — leading to a distribution that is narrow, repetitive, and lower quality than the original human-generated data. This is an active research concern in the AI safety and data provenance community. A (catastrophic forgetting) refers to forgetting previously learned tasks during fine-tuning on new data. C (overfitting) is a traditional ML phenomenon unrelated to iterative synthetic training. D (reward hacking) applies to reinforcement learning from human feedback, not synthetic pretraining.
questions.reference: Responsible AI: model collapse risk with synthetic data

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team implements a parent-document retrieval strategy: documents are split into small child chunks (128 tokens) for embedding and retrieval, but when a child chunk is matched, the full parent document section (1024 tokens) is passed to the LLM. What is the PRIMARY benefit of this approach over simply indexing 1024-token chunks directly?

options.text: Smaller child chunks produce more precise embeddings that match queries accurately, while the larger parent context gives the LLM sufficient surrounding information to generate a complete answer
Parent-document retrieval reduces the number of vectors stored in the index, lowering storage costs
Parent-document retrieval eliminates the need for chunk overlap, simplifying the ingestion pipeline
The parent document provides the LLM with the model card and metadata for the document, not the actual text

questions.explanation: A is correct here because this strategy exploits a key tension: small chunks embed well (a 128-token chunk is focused and topically tight, producing a highly relevant embedding), but they lack the surrounding context needed for the LLM to produce a complete, coherent answer. By retrieving the matching child chunk for embedding comparison but returning the parent chunk to the LLM, both goals are achieved simultaneously. B is wrong — parent-document retrieval actually stores MORE vectors (all the small child chunks), not fewer. C is wrong; overlap is a separate concern and is still useful for child chunks. D is incorrect; the parent document is the surrounding text section, not metadata.
questions.reference: RAG architecture: parent-document retrieval strategy

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team logs MLflow traces for their LangChain RAG chain using mlflow.langchain.autolog(). In the MLflow UI, they see a span labeled 'ChatDatabricks' with very high duration values. What does this indicate?

options.text: The vector search retrieval step is slow and should be optimized
The LLM inference call (ChatDatabricks) is taking a long time, possibly due to a large input prompt, long generated output, or endpoint capacity constraints
The embedding model is timing out during document loading
MLflow tracing overhead is causing the performance degradation

questions.explanation: B is correct here because the 'ChatDatabricks' span in an MLflow trace corresponds to the LLM inference call made via the ChatDatabricks LangChain class. High duration for this span indicates the LLM call itself is slow — this could be caused by a very long input context (more tokens to process), a long generated response, high endpoint utilization, or an undersized serving endpoint. A is wrong because vector search retrieval would appear in a separate span. C is wrong because embedding during query time is a separate operation. D is wrong because MLflow tracing overhead is minimal (sub-millisecond) and would not appear as the dominant span duration.
questions.reference: MLflow tracing for LLMs: tracing spans

questions.stem: In this scenario, a QA engineer wants to write automated tests for a LangChain RAG chain that assert the output is deterministic and consistent. Which LLM parameter setting is REQUIRED for the test to be reproducible across runs?

options.text: Set max_tokens=100 to limit response length and make comparisons easier
Set temperature=0 so the LLM always selects the highest-probability token, producing deterministic output for the same input
Set top_p=0.95 to reduce stochasticity during testing
Disable the retrieval step and test the LLM in isolation from the vector index

questions.explanation: B is correct here because at temperature=0 the LLM uses greedy decoding — it always selects the single highest-probability next token at each step, making the output fully deterministic for a given input. This is the standard approach for making LLM outputs reproducible in automated tests. Note that very minor floating-point differences across hardware can occasionally produce different results even at temperature=0, but for practical testing purposes it provides determinism. A (max_tokens) limits length but does not make generation deterministic within that length. C (top_p=0.95) still samples from the top 95% of the distribution and produces non-deterministic output. D (disabling retrieval) does not test the actual chain behavior.
questions.reference: Application development: testing LLM apps with deterministic prompts (temperature=0)

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to enable Databricks Vector Search Delta Sync on a Delta table. When they try to create the index, they receive an error saying the source table is not supported. What is the MOST likely reason?

options.text: The table is in a different database schema than the Vector Search endpoint
The Delta table does not have Change Data Feed (CDF) enabled, which is required for Delta Sync to track incremental changes
The table contains more than 1 million rows, exceeding the Delta Sync limit
The table uses ZORDER optimization, which is incompatible with Vector Search indexing

questions.explanation: B is correct here because Databricks Vector Search Delta Sync requires the source Delta table to have Change Data Feed (CDF) enabled (ALTER TABLE table_name SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.enableChangeDataFeed' = 'true')). Without CDF, the sync pipeline cannot track which rows have been inserted, updated, or deleted since the last sync. A is wrong because cross-schema access is supported given appropriate permissions. C is wrong because there is no documented row count limit that small for Delta Sync indexes. D is wrong because ZORDER optimization is compatible with Vector Search; it only affects the physical layout of the Delta table files for Spark queries.
questions.reference: Delta tables for vector indexing: schema requirements

questions.stem: In this scenario, a data science team wants to build a code assistant that can answer questions about a company's internal proprietary codebase of 500,000 lines of code. The codebase is stored in a private Git repository and changes daily. Which approach is MOST suitable?

options.text: Fine-tune an LLM on the full codebase once per quarter
Build a RAG pipeline that indexes code files and docstrings in a vector store, refreshed nightly from the repository
Paste the entire codebase into the LLM's context window on every query
Use a general-purpose coding LLM without any additional codebase context

questions.explanation: B is correct here because RAG allows the system to retrieve only the relevant code snippets for each query from the indexed codebase, keeping knowledge current with nightly refreshes and avoiding the need to retrain. A is wrong because quarterly fine-tuning would miss daily changes and is expensive. C is wrong because 500,000 lines of code far exceeds any current context window limit. D is wrong because a general-purpose LLM has no knowledge of proprietary internal codebases.
questions.reference: RAG Architecture: when to use RAG vs fine-tuning

questions.stem: A developer is configuring a Databricks Vector Search retriever in LangChain and wants to filter results to only include documents from a specific product category stored as metadata. Select TWO correct statements about metadata filtering in Databricks Vector Search.

options.text: Metadata filters are applied after the ANN search, reducing the candidate set returned to the application
Metadata filters can be passed as a dictionary to the filters parameter in similarity_search()
Metadata columns used for filtering must be specified when the index is created
Metadata filtering is not supported in Databricks Vector Search; all filtering must be done in post-processing

questions.explanation: B is correct here because the Databricks Vector Search client's similarity_search() method accepts a filters parameter (as a dictionary of column:value pairs) that restricts results to documents matching those metadata criteria. C is correct because filterable metadata columns must be declared when the index is created — they cannot be added as filter targets arbitrarily at query time. A is partially misleading — pre-filtering (before ANN) and post-filtering both exist, but Databricks Vector Search applies filters differently depending on index configuration. D is wrong because metadata filtering is a native, documented feature of Databricks Vector Search.
questions.reference: Databricks Vector Search API: filters with metadata

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team tracks the RAGAS context_precision metric for their RAG application and observes it dropping over time. What does this trend indicate about the system's behavior?

options.text: The generated answers are becoming less faithful to the retrieved context
An increasing proportion of retrieved chunks are not relevant to the user's query — the retriever is returning more noise alongside useful chunks
The context window of the LLM is being exceeded more frequently
The embedding model is degrading due to data drift in incoming queries

questions.explanation: B is correct here because context_precision = (number of relevant chunks in top-k) / (total chunks in top-k). A decreasing context_precision means that over time, a higher fraction of the retrieved chunks are not actually relevant to the user's question — retrieval quality is degrading. This could be caused by index data drift (new documents added that are topically similar but semantically different from the query), changes in query patterns (users asking new types of questions that the embedding space doesn't handle well), or index quality issues. A describes faithfulness metric degradation. C is an inference constraint, not a RAGAS metric. D is a plausible root cause but 'embedding model degrading' is not what context_precision directly measures.
questions.reference: RAGAS evaluation metrics: context precision

questions.stem: A team needs to build a customer support chatbot that answers questions about their product documentation. The documentation is updated weekly. Which approach is MOST appropriate for keeping the chatbot's knowledge current without retraining a model?

options.text: Fine-tune an open-source LLM on the documentation corpus each week
Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with a vector index refreshed from the updated documentation
Include the full documentation in every prompt to the LLM
Use a proprietary LLM with a large context window so no retrieval is needed

questions.explanation: B is correct here because RAG decouples the knowledge base from the model weights, allowing the vector index to be refreshed with new documents without retraining. A is wrong because fine-tuning weekly is expensive and slow, and fine-tuned knowledge can still become stale. C is wrong because documentation corpora are typically too large to fit in any context window, and cost per query would be prohibitive. D is wrong because even large context windows cannot hold enterprise documentation, and the model's parametric knowledge would still be static.
questions.reference: RAG Architecture: retriever + generator pattern

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team uses the Databricks VectorSearchClient to query their index. They call client.get_index('catalog.schema.my_index').similarity_search(query_text='What is the refund policy?', columns=['id', 'text', 'department'], num_results=5). What does the num_results parameter control?

options.text: The maximum number of tokens in each returned document chunk
The number of nearest neighbor chunks returned by the vector search (top-k)
The number of parallel search threads used by the index
The number of columns returned in the result set

questions.explanation: B is correct here because num_results in the Databricks Vector Search API's similarity_search() method specifies the number of most similar chunks to return (equivalent to top-k in nearest neighbor search terminology). Increasing num_results returns more candidate chunks but also increases the amount of context passed to the LLM. A is wrong because token limits per chunk are determined at indexing time. C is wrong because parallelism is an internal index configuration, not a per-query parameter. D is wrong because the columns returned are controlled by the 'columns' parameter, not 'num_results'.
questions.reference: Databricks Vector Search API: similarity_search()

questions.stem: In this scenario, an engineer is designing an embedding strategy for a RAG system that will search across both scientific papers and short news headlines. The engineer considers using a single embedding model for both. What is the PRIMARY risk of this approach?

options.text: The embedding model may have a maximum token limit that excludes full scientific papers
A single generic embedding model may not capture domain-specific terminology from scientific literature, leading to poor retrieval quality for technical queries
Using one embedding model means the system cannot support cosine similarity search
A single model increases the index size unnecessarily

questions.explanation: B is correct here because a generic embedding model (e.g., trained on web text) may not adequately represent domain-specific vocabulary and concepts from scientific literature, resulting in poor semantic matching for technical queries. Domain-specific or specialized embedding models (e.g., BioBERT for biomedical text) produce better representations for specialized domains. A is a real technical constraint (max token limits exist) but it is addressed by chunking, not by using multiple models. C is wrong because the similarity metric is independent of the embedding model count. D is wrong because using one model does not affect index size — index size depends on the number of vectors and their dimensionality.
questions.reference: Embeddings: domain-specific embedding considerations

questions.stem: A team is designing a production RAG system. Select TWO design decisions that directly affect the latency of the retrieval step (before the LLM call).

options.text: The number of chunks retrieved (top-k value)
The temperature parameter set on the LLM endpoint
Whether the vector index uses approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) or exact search
The number of few-shot examples in the system prompt

questions.explanation: A is correct here because a higher top-k requires the vector search engine to score more candidates and return a larger result set, increasing retrieval latency. C is correct because exact nearest neighbor search scans all vectors and is O(n), while ANN algorithms (e.g., HNSW) use approximations to achieve sub-linear search time at the cost of perfect recall. B is wrong because the LLM temperature only affects the generation step, not retrieval. D is wrong because few-shot examples are part of the LLM prompt and only affect the generation step's latency (more input tokens to process), not the vector retrieval step.
questions.reference: Vector search design: approximate vs exact search; RAG architecture

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team is designing a multi-agent GenAI system where a 'router' LLM decides which specialist agent (search agent, calculator agent, or document summarizer) should handle each user query. What is the PRIMARY benefit of this architecture over a single monolithic LLM?

options.text: Multi-agent systems always produce better answers because multiple models vote on the response

Each specialist agent can be optimized independently for its specific task — using the right model size, tool access, and prompt template — resulting in better performance and cost efficiency than a single generalist LLM trying to do everything
Multi-agent systems eliminate hallucination because agents can check each other's work
Multi-agent systems require no RAG because each agent has its own fine-tuned knowledge

questions.explanation: B is correct here because decomposing a complex task into specialized sub-agents allows each component to be optimized independently. A search agent might use a small, fast model with vector search; a calculator agent might use a code-execution tool with a deterministic engine; a summarizer might use a larger model with a long context window. This is more efficient than forcing a single model to handle all task types with a single prompt strategy. A is wrong because ensemble voting is not inherent to multi-agent architecture. C is wrong because individual agents still hallucinate; peer review between agents reduces but does not eliminate errors. D is wrong because individual agents may still need RAG for knowledge-intensive sub-tasks.
questions.reference: RAG architecture: GenAI application design patterns

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team observes that their RAG application's average query cost is $0.018 per query, broken down as: embedding ($0.001) + vector search ($0.002) + LLM inference ($0.015). They want to reduce overall cost by 30% without degrading answer quality. Which lever provides the LARGEST cost reduction opportunity?

options.text: Switch to a cheaper embedding model to reduce the embedding cost from $0.001 to $0.0005
Reduce the LLM inference cost by switching to a smaller or more cost-efficient model, or by optimizing the prompt to reduce input token count — since LLM inference represents 83% of the total cost
Cache vector search results for identical query embeddings to eliminate the $0.002 vector search cost for repeated queries
Increase top-k from 3 to 5 to improve answer quality, which will allow the team to use a cheaper LLM

questions.explanation: B is correct here because LLM inference at $0.015 represents 83% of the $0.018 total cost. A 30% reduction in total cost ($0.0054) requires reducing the largest cost component. Switching from a large expensive model to a smaller model with equivalent performance, or reducing the system prompt length and retrieved context tokens, directly targets this dominant cost. A is wrong — eliminating the entire embedding cost would only save $0.001, a 5.6% total reduction, far below the 30% goal. C is wrong — caching vector search would save at most $0.002 (11%), and only for repeated identical queries. D is counterproductive — increasing top-k increases LLM input tokens and therefore increases the very cost the team wants to reduce.
questions.reference: Maintaining applications: cost per query decomposition (embedding + vector search + LLM tokens)

questions.stem: A team decomposes the cost of each query to their RAG application. Select TWO components that contribute to the per-query LLM inference cost.

options.text: The number of input tokens (system prompt + retrieved context + user query)
The number of vectors stored in the vector index
The number of output tokens generated by the LLM
The dimensionality of the embedding vectors in the index

questions.explanation: A is correct here because pay-per-token pricing for LLM APIs (and provisioned throughput calculations) charges for both input tokens and output tokens. Input tokens include the system prompt, all retrieved context chunks, and the user's query — this can be several thousand tokens for a RAG system with a large system prompt. C is correct because output tokens (the generated response) are also billed and can vary significantly depending on the complexity and length of the answer. B is wrong because vector index storage is a separate infrastructure cost (per-vector storage fee), not a per-query inference cost. D is wrong because embedding vector dimensionality affects index storage size but not LLM inference cost.
questions.reference: Maintaining applications: cost per query decomposition (embedding + vector search + LLM tokens)

questions.stem: In this scenario, a PDF corpus contains financial reports where key figures appear inside tables rather than in prose. A team uses a naive text extraction method (pdfminer) and finds the LLM frequently misquotes numbers from these tables. What is the MOST reliable fix?

options.text: Increase the top-k from 3 to 10 so more chunks are retrieved, increasing the chance the correct number appears in one of them

Use a table-aware extraction library such as Camelot or pdfplumber that identifies table cells and reconstructs row/column structure, then serialize each table as a Markdown table or CSV before indexing
Post-process the LLM output with a regex to correct any numbers that appear to be misquoted
Fine-tune the LLM on the financial reports so it memorizes the key figures

questions.explanation: B is correct here because naive text extractors like pdfminer read the PDF character stream, which for tables often yields garbled column ordering, merged cells, or wrong row associations — leading the LLM to misquote figures. Table-aware parsers (Camelot for grid-line tables, pdfplumber for borderless tables) reconstruct the actual tabular structure, allowing each table to be serialized as a readable Markdown table or CSV. Indexing this serialized form gives the LLM the correct row/column context. A increases retrieval breadth but does not fix the corrupted table text. C post-processing cannot reliably identify which numbers are wrong. D fine-tuning on specific figures is brittle and expensive and will fail when reports are updated.
questions.reference: Data preparation: handling PDF tables (Camelot, pdfplumber vs naive text extraction)

questions.stem: In this scenario, a developer needs to implement a conversational RAG system where follow-up questions like 'What about the second point?' implicitly refer to the previous response. How should the retrieval query be formulated for follow-up questions?

options.text: Use only the follow-up question as the vector search query, ignoring conversation history
Concatenate all previous turns into a single long query for the vector search
Use a 'query reformulation' step where an LLM rewrites the follow-up question into a standalone question using conversation history, then use that as the retrieval query
Disable retrieval for follow-up questions and rely on the LLM's parametric memory

questions.explanation: C is correct here because follow-up questions like 'What about the second point?' are not self-contained — they require the prior context to be meaningful as a search query. A query reformulation step (sometimes called 'contextualized query generation') uses a fast LLM call to rewrite the follow-up as a standalone question ('What is the second recommendation from the product roadmap described earlier?'), which then produces a much better embedding for vector search. A is wrong because 'What about the second point?' as a standalone query will retrieve poor results. B is wrong because concatenating all turns creates a long, noisy query that dilutes the specific intent of the follow-up. D is wrong because the LLM's parametric memory does not contain information from the retrieved documents.
questions.reference: Multi-turn conversations: conversation history management

questions.stem: In this scenario, a team uses Databricks Vector Search with a pre-filter metadata strategy. A document has metadata field `region='EMEA'` and a query uses `filters={'region': 'EMEA'}`. With pre-filtering (filter before ANN search), what is a potential drawback compared to post-filtering?

options.text: Pre-filtering always returns zero results because it eliminates all documents before the search
Pre-filtering can significantly reduce the candidate set before ANN search, which may result in fewer than the requested top-k results if very few documents match the metadata filter
Pre-filtering is not supported in Databricks Vector Search and always falls back to post-filtering
Pre-filtering increases latency because it must scan every document in the index before running ANN

questions.explanation: B is correct here because pre-filtering narrows the candidate pool before the ANN search runs. If a metadata filter is highly selective (e.g., only 50 documents out of 1,000,000 have `region='EMEA'`), the ANN search operates on a very small subset, potentially returning fewer than the requested top-k results and degrading retrieval quality. Post-filtering runs ANN on all vectors first and then filters by metadata, which avoids this sparsity problem but may return fewer usable results if many ANN results are filtered out. Understanding this trade-off is important when designing filtered vector search. A is wrong because pre-filtering does not eliminate all documents. C is wrong because Databricks Vector Search supports both approaches. D is wrong because pre-filtering reduces the search space and thus decreases, not increases, ANN latency.
questions.reference: Data preparation: metadata filtering during vector search (pre-filter vs post-filter)

questions.stem: In this scenario, a developer wants to stream LLM responses to the user interface token-by-token to reduce perceived latency, instead of waiting for the full response to be generated. Which Databricks Model Serving feature enables this?

options.text: Enable streaming by setting stream=True in the request payload; the endpoint returns a Server-Sent Events (SSE) stream of token chunks
Configure the endpoint with min_provisioned_throughput=1 to enable token-level streaming
Use the Databricks SQL ai_query() function with the STREAM keyword to receive tokens one at a time
Set temperature=0 on the endpoint to enable deterministic streaming mode

questions.explanation: A is correct here because Databricks Model Serving endpoints that host chat/completion models support streaming responses via the Server-Sent Events (SSE) protocol, which is the same standard used by OpenAI's streaming API. The caller sets `stream=True` in the request body (following the OpenAI API convention), and the endpoint returns a stream of delta objects, each containing the next token or tokens as they are generated. B is wrong because provisioned throughput affects capacity, not the streaming protocol. C is wrong because ai_query() is a batch SQL function and does not support streaming. D is wrong because temperature affects generation diversity, not streaming behavior.
questions.reference: Application development: streaming responses from Model Serving (SSE)

questions.stem: In this scenario, a Databricks engineer creates a Direct Vector Access index in Databricks Vector Search. How does this index type differ from a Delta Sync index in terms of data management?

options.text: A Direct Vector Access index automatically syncs with a source Delta table; a Delta Sync index requires manual API calls to update
A Direct Vector Access index requires the application to explicitly upsert and delete vectors via API; a Delta Sync index automatically tracks changes in the source Delta table
A Direct Vector Access index only supports exact search; a Delta Sync index supports approximate nearest neighbor search
A Direct Vector Access index stores raw text; a Delta Sync index stores pre-computed embeddings

questions.explanation: B is correct here and accurately describes the two index types. A Direct Vector Access index is managed by the application — you call the API to upsert or delete individual vectors, giving you fine-grained control. A Delta Sync index is managed by a pipeline that automatically watches for changes in the source Delta table (via Change Data Feed) and propagates them to the index. A is the reverse of the correct description. C is wrong because the search algorithm (ANN vs exact) is configurable independently of index type. D is wrong because both index types can store embeddings; the distinction is about update management.
questions.reference: Vector search design: Databricks Vector Search index types

questions.stem: In this scenario, a developer is designing a prompt template for a customer-facing RAG chatbot. The system prompt should ensure the model stays focused on product support topics. Which system prompt pattern is MOST effective?

options.text: You are a helpful assistant. Answer any question the user asks.

You are a product support assistant for Acme Corp. Answer questions using ONLY the provided context. If the question is not about Acme products or is not addressed in the context, respond: 'I can only help with Acme product questions. Please contact support@acme.com for other inquiries.'
You are an AI. Never make mistakes.
Answer the question. Do not use the context.

questions.explanation: B is correct here because it specifies: (1) the assistant's role and company context, (2) the instruction to use ONLY the provided context (grounding), and (3) a clear fallback behavior for out-of-scope questions with a specific response template. This prevents topic drift, reduces hallucination, and provides users with a useful next step when the bot cannot help. A is too broad — 'any question' invites off-topic queries and hallucination. C contains vague, unenforceable instructions. D actively instructs the model to ignore the retrieved context, undermining the purpose of RAG.
questions.reference: Prompt templates: system prompts
